title: Primary and Secondary School
description: Coming from an all-girls school, I did not experience discrimination but rather a strong sense of women’s empowerment. I lived like a typical high schooler, dealing with the usual teenage challenges. I was able to express myself fully, especially in sports. Being in an all-girls environment allowed me to appreciate the strength that comes from women supporting one another, and it opened me to the fluidity of identity and gender.

place: College of Social Science and Philosophy, UP Diliman
title: Undergraduate Studies
description: Being exposed to people from diverse backgrounds in UP Diliman allowed me to broaden my perspective on women’s lived experiences. I learned a lot on struggles associated to gender from classrooms and beyond -- being surrounded by activist groups—who actively fight for equality, not only for women but also for other marginalized communities such as LGBTQIA+, Indigenous peoples, and the labor sector.

title: Exchange Program
description: In a foreign classroom, surrounded by women from across the world, I found a shared understanding: we were all expected to prove ourselves just a little more. Despite our different cultures, the pattern was familiar—men in positions of power, women working twice as hard to be seen. It made me realize that gendered expectations travel across borders, quietly shaping our ambitions.

title: GIS Analyst
description: Seeing a female leader manage a team of more than ten male developers gave me hope. Although she was not my direct supervisor, I looked up to her as someone who stood out in a male-dominated field. She inspired me to work harder and remain unfazed by gender inequalities in the workplace.

title: GIS Specialist
description:
In Saudi Arabia, I became acutely aware of what it meant to occupy space as the only woman in the room. For the first time, I worked in an office building without a designated women’s restroom, forcing me to leave the building and use facilities in another one, where access was limited to local women and authorized personnel. After transferring to a different space from Dammam to Khobar, I encountered the same issue—this time having to go to the third floor of a different building just to use a restroom.

I was also surprised to learn from colleagues that local female employees had only started being accepted in the office after 2020. Over time, the awkward stares from men in the hallways became less frequent. While I generally felt safe in a country with laws rooted in Islamic principles that emphasize respect for women, being a foreign woman of a different religion made me feel like an outsider. Visible differences—such as not wearing a hijab, along with my skin color and facial features—often marked me as “other” at first glance.

title: MS Geomatics Engineering - Geoinformatics
description: Returning home felt different this time. What once seemed ordinary—access, inclusion, opportunity—now carried new meaning. As I continue my studies, I find myself more aware of the spaces I move through and the privileges they hold. In coming back, I am not the same person who left—and the city reflects that change back to me.
